I would like to add some larger computational evidence for the exceptional integers in this problem. Recall that $n$ is representable in the form\[ n=ap^2+b,\qquad p\ \text{prime},\quad a\geq 1,\quad 0\leq b

100000078386123. \]For\[ n=10000001000000006 \]we have\[ \lfloor \sqrt n\rfloor = 100000004. \]The computation checks every prime $p\leq 100000004$ and finds that\[ n\bmod p^2 \geq p \]in every case. Therefore this $n$ has no representation of the required form. Some further exceptions found in the same range are\[ \begin{aligned} 10000001000000006,\quad 10000001000000010,\quad 10000001000000011,\quad 10000001000000014,\quad 10000001000000015,\\ 10000001000000019,\quad 10000001000000022,\quad 10000001000000023,\quad 10000001000952655,\quad 10000001000952662,\\ 10000001000952663,\quad 10000001000952670,\quad 10000001000952671,\quad 10000001000952674,\quad 10000001000952690,\\ 10000001000952691,\quad 10000001000952698,\quad 10000001000952699,\quad 10000001000952706,\quad 10000001000952707. \end{aligned} \]These examples suggest defining the exceptional sequence\[ E=\{n\in\mathbb N:\ n\bmod p^2\geq p \text{ for every prime }p\leq \sqrt n\}. \]Equivalently, if $E=\{e_1

R. Zeraouila has shown that the exceptional set in this problem is finite, conditional on Strong Uniformity Hypothesis which further follows from either the Elliott-Halberstam conjecture or the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis in the preprint Entropy—Sieve Methods and Energy Functionals in the Erdős Problem [Er79] on Quadratic Prime Representations(2025).

I ran the paper (which is labeled as "not peer reviewed") through ChatGPT Pro and it flagged some potential issues with it. I would hold off on viewing it as confirmed until it is accepted by a reputable journal.

Dear Professor Alfaiz, Thank you very much for your encouraging comment and for your careful reading of the preprint. I truly appreciate your positive assessment and your interest in the conditional result concerning the finiteness of the exceptional set under the Strong Uniformity Hypothesis. Your feedback is very motivating, and I am grateful for your support as I continue refining the work and strengthening the presentation. With sincere thanks and best regards, Rafik

In a highly experimental use, I have tried with ChatGPT to assess the direction of the preprint (without judging the author one way or the other). I realize this more of an art than science... First, we discussed the general background, such as the "almost all" result and known sieve heuristics. The main obstacle is identified: transitioning from "rare" to "impossible". The paper's strategy is identified as studying the events $E_p(n) := \{ n \mod p^2 < p \}$ with vector of indicators $X_p(n) := 1_{E_p(n)}$. We want to compare the true joint law $P$ of $(X_p)_{p \leq X}$ to the product law $Q = \prod P_p$. The quantity to control is Kullback-Leibler divergence $D(P \parallel Q)$. The author then posited the Uniformity Hypothesis (UH) and strong Uniformity Hypothesis (sUH) which are bounds on $D(P \parallel Q)$. In particular, sUH is of importance that it should imply the finiteness of the exceptional set $E$ (Claim 1). Moreover, the author claims that either the Elliott-Halberstam conjecture or generalized Riemann Hypothesis imply sUH (Claim 2). We then see that Claims 1 and 2 are the two main pieces to assess. After thinking for over 15 minutes, ChatGPT assessed the two claims as follows. $\textbf{Claim 1}$. The main claim here is Theorem 6.9, whose proof is only 4 lines (!) and invokes Borel-Cantelli. ChatGPT assessed the direction of the proof as: "- Even a very strong average bound like $\Pr_{n\le x}(n\in E)\ll 1/(\log x)^{1+\varepsilon}$ would not by itself imply the deterministic set $E$ is finite; it would only say it’s sparse on average. The paper’s proof sketch invokes Borel–Cantelli in a way that (as written) seems to apply to a random sequence of sampled integers, not to the actual set of all exceptions. - More fundamentally: with “independence-like” behavior, the heuristic size of the exceptional set is typically $\sim x/\log x$ (because $\prod_{p\le X}(1-1/p)\sim e^{-\gamma}/\log X$). Independence pushes you toward infinitely many exceptions, not finiteness. The paper is trying to conclude the opposite from an “almost-independence” hypothesis, which is already a big red flag relative to the mechanism it sets up." Moreover, the proof of Theorem 6.9 claims bounds $\ll 1/(\log X)^{1+\varepsilon}$. ChatGPT sketched arguments that the actual machinery should give $\ll 1/(\log X)^{1-\varepsilon}$; even under "perfect independence" that'd only get you $\ll 1/\log X$. Conclusion: the claimed exponent appears too strong from existing machinery, and, even if we do get this exponent, it's only an average bound and that doesn't bridge to finiteness. Moreover, the "almost-independence" heuristic seems to be going in the wrong direction. $\textbf{Claim 2}$. The implication from EH/GRH to sUH. ChatGPT argued this is problematic for a "simpler reason": the $N(x;q,b)$ is defined as the count of integers in a residue class. Since these counts and their second moments are already elementary, we do not need EH/GRH to control them. In fact, EH/GRH is about primes in arithmetic progressions and likely irrelevant here. In ChatGPT's words: "“EH/GRH ⇒ strong second-moment control for primes in progressions” is true in the right context, and “second-moment control ⇒ small KL divergence” is a real information-theoretic theme (they even state a χ²/KL control via an energy functional). But the paper’s specific EH/GRH ⇒ sUH claim doesn’t line up with the objects it defined (integer residue counts), and the modulus/scale issues are not resolved." Conclusion: there could be some right conceptual ideas, but the connections to EH/GRH are likely irrelevant. $\textbf{Overall}$. It seems to me that both claims are difficult to fix. EH/GRH is not likely to get to Uniformity because they don't seem to apply to this setting; on the other hand, Uniformity also doesn't seem to get to finiteness due to the kind of argument used. As always, I write from my best understanding and do not claim accuracy in the chat's statements.

Greeting! I would like to share a recent attempt on [79] (quadratic prime representations). In my preprint I introduce an **Entropy--Sieve Method**, which combines sieve theory with information-theoretic tools (relative entropy/KL divergence and a quadratic energy functional). The key reduction shows that finiteness of the exceptional set may follow if one can establish the uniformity estimate\[ E_2(P) \;=\; \frac{1+o(1)}{M}, \qquad M=\prod_{p\le Y} p^2, \]for the quadratic energy of the residue distribution. A change-of-measure argument via KL divergence then transforms this into a sharp tail bound for the sieve sum. What is important here is that this framework isolates precisely the analytic input needed: if the above uniformity holds, then the \emph{exceptional set} $E$ (integers with no quadratic prime representation) is finite. In other words, only finitely many large integers could fail Erdős's representation. This directly addresses the central question of [79]. Our numerical experiments further confirm that many large numbers remain outside the representable set, showing that unconditional tools do not yet shrink $E$ to finite --- highlighting both the sharpness of our reduction and the difficulty of the problem. Thus, our contribution is twofold: (i) it provides a conditional path to prove finiteness of $E$ under standard distributional conjectures (EH or GRH), and (ii) it clarifies why unconditional finiteness is still out of reach, since in practice large integers without representation persist far beyond current analytic barriers. \textbf{Preprint:} ResearchGate link, DOI: 10.13140/RG.2.2.14599.05283. I would be especially grateful for feedback on two delicate analytic points: 1. The explicit derivation and control of the divisor-weights $w(q,M)$ in the variance decomposition. 2. The uniform lower bound $Q_r \gg 1/M$ required when passing from $\chi^2$ divergence to KL divergence. Any references or suggestions regarding these aspects, or about related treatments of primes modulo $p^2$, would be very welcome.

Related to the setting of Selfridge and Wagstaff where the condition of primes isn't there; If one considers $\prod_{2 \le x \le \sqrt n} (1-1/x) =1/\sqrt(n)$, then under some intuitive independence of chances, similar as above, one expects roughly $2n^{1/2}$ numbers up to $n$ that are not of the desired form. Since one may expect some positive correlation (if it is small modulo $16^2$, it is with chances $1/4$ small $32^2$), $2n^{1/2}$ is even a lower bound. I think it is these type of estimates one wants to make rigourous, or at least a lower bound on the number of solutions not of that form, and not a particular construction. For $c_n$; $c_n>3/2$ does not make sense allowing $p=2$, so there should be a more subtle question there, where the bound on $b$ is e.g. a $p^{1+o(1)}$ bound.

Indeed, there is no doubt that the answer is negative. Good candidates for numbers that cannot be expressed as such are 3, 35, 899, and in general any number that is one less than than the square of a primorial. Any ideas as to how to prove this rigorously?

For $p$ small, and $n$ arbitrary, the chances that $n \pmod {p^2} $<$p$ (representant in $[0,p^2[$) is $\frac 1p.$ Thus for large $n$ arbitrary, the chance could be guessed to be (assuming some independence of the chances) $$\prod_{p \le \sqrt n} (1-1/p) \sim 2\exp(-\gamma)/ \log n.$$ The sieve methods verify one side of this intuition in rough sense. For a fixed value of $n$, chances are small. But ranging over intervals, many solutions can be expected. In particular, hte proposed numbers are not that much better than others (only slightly better). All you know is that for $(p_k\#)^2-1$, there is such form involving a prime $p_i$ with $i\le k$ (but many larger primes remain) So just as confirmation that the primorials do not work that often; $n=(11\#)^2-1=5336099$ satisfies $n \equiv 3\pmod{289}$ , $n=(17\#)^2-1 \equiv 7159 \pmod{16651^2}$ and $n=(19\#)^2-1=94083986096099 \equiv 2133 \pmod{3469^2}.$ The next ones fail modulo $31^2, 211^2, 353^2$ and $197^2$ resp., and so do many larger ones.
